speaker: interviewee
text: [SIDE CONVERSATION]

speaker: interviewer
text: Today is November 8, 1994. The survivor is Hart, Sigi. The interviewer is Deborah Kattler. The city is Los Angeles, and the language is English. You could go.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Sigi Hart. I was born in Berlin, 1925-- November, 1925. And stop.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's fine. You're doing fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah?

speaker: interviewer
text: It's OK. Yeah. It was perfect.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Berlin and grew up there until my school time when I went to school first grade.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little bit about your family. Tell me about your parents, and your brother, and your sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents, my mother, Adela. We were Hartmeyer in Berlin. Actually first we were Begleiter, and later on, the German government, they create a law-- my parents are from Poland, they're not Germans-- that they have to come to present themselves, and they had to show papers. And the German government did not recognize the marriage by rabbinate, from the rabbi. So my father had to take the name of his mother. So we were Begleiter until about 1935, and then we became Hartmeyer. So I started school with Begleiter, in 1931, and went to first grade and second grade as Begleiter. I have a sister, who her name is Mania. And I have a younger brother whose name, we called him Willie, Wolf. And we had a happy life. As children, my parents were good to us. We had a good life. My mother was a housewife. My dad was a traveling salesperson-- man, and was a good provider. I cannot complain as a child. We had a pretty good life. And it started in 1933. Hitler came to power and started first with the Communists. Trouble then started with the Jews. And I felt already in school, when I went to school, they put me in the last row of the class, because I was Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you when you first noticed that you were treated differently?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was seven years old. This was my first, second grade going to school. It started. I looked fairly dark. I don't look like a real German, blue eyes and blond. I was beaten up in school by the youngsters, and I was afraid to go to school. So my father decided my mother was born in Oswiecim. This became Auschwitz, later infamous place, to go to Oswiecim to visit her grandmother. A lot of family lived in Oswiecim, so our family went to Oswiecim. We stayed there about a year, and we picked up a little bit of the Polish language. I started school in a village, and it was pretty nice. We had a lot of family there-- cousins and uncles. And we stayed there till 1934, and my dad decided that it calmed down in Berlin. We should come back. We did not believe that really it will grow to something big, this Hitler. So we came back to Berlin, and my parents put me in a Jewish boys' school. It was called Kaiserstrasse. And we lived pretty much in the center of Berlin, in a Jewish neighborhood. And I went there to school. It was a boys' school, and we were about 800 boys in this school. Later, my brother went to the same school.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we still continued fairly quietly. We were taught not to make any noise in the street, not to be seen, not to be recognized as Jews. What do you mean recognize?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were children. We were playing. We were shouting. We were doing things, and our parents always said, psht. Don't do it. Don't be remarked by the goyim. You know, they will beat you up. And it was really like this. When we went to school, we went three, four children. From my home to the school was about a 40 minutes walk. We walked to school. We left the house at 7:00. We went at a quarter to 8:00, and then school from 8:00 till 2 o'clock. After school, we could not play in the street, because Jews were beaten up. We went to a big synagogue next to our house, what was called the Oranienburg Temple. There was a class like where we can come after school to make our homework, to play chess, to play ping pong, to do a little sport in the back of the temple, just not to be in the street. Everything to be fading out from the streets, not to be seen. And it got worse. Every time, we couldn't go to parks. They started writing on the benches in 1935. They wrote on the benches, [GERMAN]. It means not for Jews. We could not sit on the benches in the street. We were not allowed to go to parks. We had the special place where we could go, and this was a little park about an hour out from Berlin. And the Jewish youngsters did some sport on the weekends. My dad worked as a salesman. He came home once a week. He left Monday and came back Friday. My mother took care of us. She was a good cook, good baker. She baked her own challahs and little breads for Friday night. She made chicken soup. And my father's brother, who lived also in Berlin, came over to Shabbos for lunch. And my brother and I, we went to shul Friday night and Saturday to sing in the choir. And we had a nice family life, and just that we have to be watching out for going in the street, because when you saw somebody, a few boys coming on the sidewalk, you went down. You didn't want to face him. We went down from the sidewalk, and they passed. Sometimes they beat you. Sometimes they just shouted dirty Jew at you, and you put your head away. And if they were trying to beat you, you ran away. That's all you can do. And my uncle was pretty good. He was a violin player. He wanted me to learn to play the violin. He was also a pretty good painter painting pictures, landscapes. And I think in our family, it's a little bit artistic. [INAUDIBLE] and my sister had a beautiful voice. She always was singing very nice, and my brother and I, we were always joking around. And as a matter of fact, when my mother's brother got married in 1975, my uncle wrote a sketch for my brother and me to present at the wedding. And it was a pretty funny sketch, because we spoke in Yiddish. Usually German boys don't speak Yiddish, but we learned it. And it was a very nice success. And I'll state where my brother always had a very, very nice voice. And I never went in deeper to singing except of the choir in the temple, so we left it there. In 1937, I started to learn for my bar mitzvah. In Germany at that time, you needed at least a year to prepare for a bar mitzvah, because it is all in Hebrew, and it takes time to learn the songs, and the words, and everything. And I started to learn. Well, to come back to 1936, when the Olympic games were played in Germany, all of a sudden, from all the benches, from all the parks the signs disappeared, because there were a lot of people from all over the world coming to Berlin. So they over-painted the benches where it was written, [GERMAN] or [GERMAN], which means Jews are not allowed here. All of a sudden, it was all painted over, like nothing ever happened there. And they had on a lot of corner stores [NON-ENGLISH] papers, newspaper with grotesquely painted faces of Jews with big noses and big eyes, just to distort. This was all taken away from the streets. Berlin became a very clean, proper, nice city for a few months in 1936. We really enjoyed it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you think about the contrast of that time? You were young. You were about 10 years old at that time, 11 years old.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, 1936, I was 12 years old, 11 years old. Well, we were happy that we can go again. We did not think that after the Olympics, that everything will come back as it was. But it came bank after the Olympiad. The foreign people left, and everything was done again. [GERMAN], and the parks were not allowed for us to visit. And we went back to our temple on Oranienburg Strasse to make our homework and do our playing inside. And 1938, in November-- no, this was before November. The German government, overnight, went from home to home. They had addresses from Polish Jews and took together all the men who were born in Poland and put them in a big police station, and a day later, were deported to the Polish border. Like today I remember, we were getting ready for school. It was 7:00, 6:30 in the morning, and my dad was home. And the doorbell rang, and I ran to the door. We had a very long corridor.

speaker: interviewer
text: I ran to the door, and there were two policemen outside standing. And they asked me, is your dad home? And I was a child. I didn't know what was going on. I said yes. And they came in, and my mother came right away-- she was behind me-- and says, yes? What happened? So she says, we came for your husband. So my mother, she said, my husband is not home. So my father, in the meantime, he heard all this from the bedroom. He covered up with the cover on the other side of the bed. And my mother said, oh, my husband is home-- I mean in Berlin.

speaker: interviewee
text: He's a traveling salesman. He's home, but not in our house. He is not in the house. I don't know. The policemen came in. They were standing in front of the bed-- or they did not want to see, or they did not want to make anything out of it. They just turned around and said, Frau Hartmeyer, when your husband comes home, tell him to come to the police station. And my mother said, of course I will do that. And when they left, first of all, my mother looked at me. She could not blame me, because I am a child. But my father jumped out, got some things, got dressed, and went in-- excuse me. Our neighbors were German Jews. And he went in there and stayed there for a few days. And the next day, the people were deported to the Polish border, and my father stayed behind. I'm sorry. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] Now afterwards, a day or so, my mother and my father, we were at the neighbors.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were talking. What will happen? They will see me in the street, and then they will put me in prison.

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe it's worthwhile I should go to the police department and present myself. And they decided, after three days, most of the people were already sent to the border. My dad decided to go to the police station and present himself voluntarily. He went to the police. He packed a little suitcase, and my mother and my dad went to the police station. And we three children, we were home. We were crying. We knew that if my dad's leaving, we don't  know when we are going to see him again. And wonder, after an hour and a half about later, my mom and my dad came back, both of them. They went to the police station. And the policeman said the action is over. We took everything we took. You can stay. And my dad stayed. So this was in 1938, and must have been October or September, in 1938. In the meantime, we prepared for my bar mitzvah, what was supposed to be on-- my birthday is November 15, and my bar mitzvah was calculated a couple days-- we go according to the Torah, so it came out that the 15th was not exactly on a Saturday. So the Saturday after the 15th would be my bar mitzvah. On November 9, 1938, in the morning, all of a sudden, we went out to school, and we saw smoke and fire. In temples, in Berlin, they started like a pogrom. They started to break in the windows. There were a lot of Jewish stores. They broke the windows from the stores, and they took out the merchandise. They brought big, big picket signs. Don't buy from the Jews, and they started burning the synagogues. And so we went back to the house. We did not go to school. We stayed home, and my parents sent out my brother, who looks really like a German. He has blonde hair and blue eyes. And he went out in the street to see what's going on, came back and told us that mostly all the stores, what belongs to Jews are broken, the windows, and the merchandise was pulled out. People are walking in and out from the stores like it was their own. And the temples were burned and the Torah, and everything. And here, a week later-- this was November 9. And a week later, I am supposed to have my bar mitzvah. So the family, everybody we knew was invited to the temple to come. And so we didn't know what to do. It came down a couple days later. And the week afterward, we notified our family that we will make the bar mitzvah-- we cannot make it in a temple, because the temple was burned down and broken. There was a little house in the back from the gabbai, the person who takes care of the temple. He says, you can do it in my house. Tell your family to come. And a rabbi who was already prepared for my bar mitzvah, who knew that I will have it, was ready for it. We came Saturday morning to this place. We had about three, four people standing outside watching if they see any police, or SS, or Nazis coming, to warn us so that we can escape from the behind in the backyard.

speaker: interviewer
text: So to describe the temple-- it was a room. In one corner were the burnt Torah scrolls. They were laying on the floo-- [SOBBING] I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were laying on the floor, covered. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] I did my [HEBREW]. And I did what I had to do for my bar mitzvah. The rabbi was standing, crying. Told me never to forget-- [CRYING] This was supposed to my happiest day. Anyway, the rabbi told me. When he made the [HEBREW] he said, remember, never forget that-- [CRYING] OK. This was my bar mitzvah in 1938. We went home, and my parents decided this is not a life to continue.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my father found out there are people going over the border. We could not get a visa anywhere. My father had family in America-- New York-- cousins.

speaker: interviewee
text: He wrote before 1937 or '36 already, he wrote to America to his cousins that if it starts to be bad in Germany, if they can send them some papers to come to America. And they really did. They sent papers. They said send affidavits, what they were called. We had to wait. My parents, born in Poland, there was a quota where you cannot just get the visa and come to America. You had to wait for your quota, and this quota was at least five years. And this was 1936, '37.

speaker: interviewer
text: We had the papers, but we cannot do anything with it. We had to wait. So they tried to get visas to China, to Burma-- and then my uncle says, what are you going to go to China-- what will you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: They did not believe that it will come to a point where Jews will be killed in Germany. Excuse me. My parents decided to go to Belgium. Belgium was not giving visas to enter. But they said if you come to Belgium as a refugee, they will keep you. They will not send you back. My father found out there were people-- for money, they take you over the border. He said he will go first, and they always had the idea that the Germans wouldn't do anything to women and children. In a certain age, they didn't do anything to women and children. The proof was they took all the men away to Poland, not the women and children.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my father found out about the person and went and was smuggled into Belgium. This was after my bar mitzvah. It took a little while. We got news from him in Belgium.

speaker: interviewee
text: He went to Antwerp. He got news to us. He could not write, because he wasn't sure that they will not open the letters and see what's written.

speaker: interviewer
text: So through people, he sent us a message that he arrived in Belgium. He will make us come too. In January, 1939, we got a message from my father from Antwerp to take whatever we can carry, not too heavy. We had in Berlin a two bedroom apartment-- a nice two bedroom apartment with a bath, what was considered very nice, furnished. We had a very comfortable life.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother bought us big boots. We didn't know why. It's cold in the winter. We never had boots, and we always wanted boots. And after the bar mitzvah, my mother bought us boots. We were very happy. We thought  it was for the winter. She wouldn't tell us that we were going to Belgium. She was afraid we would tell somebody, and then we will be arrested, because we couldn't flee Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we got the boots. And in January, 1939, my mother packed a suitcase and some light bags for us kids-- for my sister, for my brother, and me-- and we closed the apartment. Whatever was in there, we closed it, went to the train station, and went to over Cologne to Aachen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Aachen is a city near the Belgium border where somebody is supposed to meet us. He described to us, you drive to Aachen. You take a little train, what goes in to a village. I remember today it was called, Rothe Erde, what means red soil-- rothe erde. And there, a smuggler will meet us and will take you over to Belgium. We came too Aachen, and we got off the train. I was sitting with my mother. We were not even all sitting together, because we didn't want to appear as a family leaving Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my mother said to my sister, my brother, you sit in the next wagon. And in Aachen, you go out. You take the little train. You will see my mother and me, and you will follow. And she didn't realize Aachen was a big city, and a lot of people came out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we went to the train to Rothe Erde, and my sister, my brother missed it. So here, my mother and I sit in the train. We're going to the border, and my sister and my brother are not there. We're going over to this little house on the border. It's a restaurant, and pension, like a little hotel on the border still in Germany. When you go out from the house, you're in Belgium. But we had to wait for a smuggler to pick us up there. It was all paid off. It was all people who made money on this. My mother and I, we were in this hotel, and it was in the afternoon. We were there till in the evening, 8 o'clock. My sister and my brother didn't come. The smuggler came, and there were another few people. We were not just my mother and I. There were another three or four grown ups going.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the smuggler came, and my mother explained what happened. He says, well, you stay, or you go with us. I don't know how she decided. She said she was going. So we started a journey at night, what was very, very, very tough.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was raining and snowing. It was in January, and we were walking in the woods. We were crossing rail stations. And after a five hour walk, it was almost 3 o'clock in the morning, the smuggler told us to stop and sit there. He's going into the next village and come back with a car.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to stop now.
